sentences.text: Yes.
We were living in the center of town.
And the Germans were clearing the center of town out of Jews.
So we moved a bit further, to our aunt-- my father's sister-- for the time being, because they said, even before the ghetto was actually built, that a ghetto will be established very soon.
The Germans.
They didn't tell this to the Jews.
But they told that to the Poles.
And the Poles told that to other Poles.
And eventually, the Jews got to know.
So we had to leave our apartment and move into [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my-- my father's sister, our auntie.
And we only could take whatever we could carry.
It was very difficult for transportation.
Poles were really not interested to assist Jews or do any donkey work for them, like helping them with a horse and carriage to move goods for them, even for money.
They really were not interested for the simple reason they had looted such a lot of Jewish wealth themselves, all of a sudden, they were all well-off, and they didn't need any help from Jews.
So whatever we could carry ourselves, we took to our aunt's place.
And the rest, we had to leave-- had to leave behind.

So we moved away from our apartment in town, moved in with our aunt, which was further down-- much further down the road, almost nearly on the borders of the actual ghetto they were erecting afterwards.
When are you talking about now?
What's the timing?
We talking about now beginning of 1940, January.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So slowly, slowly, they got the Jewish population out of the center of town and from the suburbs.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And by this opportunity, I would mention that my uncle, who lived in a fashionable part of the town, at Gdansk 28, which was a very good neighborhood, had a lovely modern apartment house, where he occupied the whole second floor.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Germans-- German officers with the insignias of the German military government came up to his apartment-- two of them.
One of them said to him, we are requisitioning your apartment as our billet.
And you will have to vacate these premises within 48 hours.
However, we two will be staying here now.
And you will have to make different arrangements for your sleeping arrangements.
And within 48 hours, you will have to leave it.
My uncle who spoke well German, having done business with Germany and been many times over to Germany, because we were living in Germany, said to these two officers, yes.
He says, it's no problem. 48 hours-- if you can give me 48 hours, we will leave.
And he gave them his two front rooms, which was the bedroom in the front and the salon, what they called, like the dining room.
These were the two front rooms.
He asked the-- the Polish maid that the beds should be arranged, because there were two officers.
That was, of course, a double bed.
And they had to get my cousin's little bed from the small room and put it into the big bedroom-- and other little alterations, which she done.
She served evening meal, because they came in the afternoon.
So she served the evening meal.
They asked the Germans what they would like to eat.
They said, oh, we got our own food.
Don't you worry, you know.
You eat what you like.
We got our own food.
And of course, they had the dining room.
And they were sitting down.
They had sausages.
They had plenty of bread.
In fact, I was there.
My uncle was there.
My brother was there-- the one who died in the war.
And they said, well, help yourself, you know.
These Germans came from a part of Czechoslovakia-- the Sudeten.
And they were in their 40s.
They were not in the Nazi party.
They were the German military.
They had a green uniform-- a light green uniform.
But they were army people.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were quite nice-- the only nice Germans I have ever come across.
So my uncle had 48 hours.
And in these 48 hours, whatever money he had, he put in a belt.
And he gave me this belt to carry.
He says, whatever happens, you know, just keep this money.
We might need it one day.
But on you, it will be safe.
And he got his suitcase ready.
He went to Warsaw, because his wife and his two children were already sent to Warsaw in advance, because my aunt had her parents and her brothers and sisters in Warsaw.
They both were dentists.
And they had their surgeries.
And the father and mother was also living there.
So he went to Warsaw.
We were still living with my aunt.
And my uncle went to Warsaw.
So he was gone.
And that was the uncle who had the large factory.
Now, the factory was taken over by ethnic German, by a foreman.
And this was done on the second day of when war broke out.
He came with a delegation of-- there was him and three others-- there were four of them, yes.
They came to my uncle.
We were there.
And they said to him-- they didn't call him Mr.
Schnitzer as they used to, but they said to him Schnitzer, without a title of Mister, give us the keys.
We are taking now possession of the factory.
We are responsible.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That is the law.
Poland is now part of Germany.
It wasn't, but they said it's part of Germany.
And they gave him a piece of paper, which he had to sign, which transferred the factory and all the goods over to these four guys.
So that was the end of the factory.
Now, as I said, my uncle went to Warsaw.
These Germans took over his apartment.
We were still living with my aunt.
And this was early January, 1940.
Then a law was passed that all the Jews still living in town must make arrangements to move into the old city, where the ghetto will be erected.
And they were already giving the plan of the streets, the locality, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how it would be supervised.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Yes.
It was at [POLISH] 31, in the old town of Lodz.
The room was about, say, if we put two people length-wise and two people in the width, I should imagine that would be, say, three meters by three.
That must have been about-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about four by four or four and a half by four and a half, which is just a small little room.
The outer house itself was of wood-- wooden construction, no water, because this was just rooms.
On each landing, you had-- one, two, three, four, five-- you had five on our landing, which was only a first floor.
You had five rooms.
And downstairs, I think you had-- one, two, three, four-- you had four.
So in our little place, you had nine rooms.
Then out in the yard, on both sides, you had about six families on one side.
And then you had the larger block, where they had also first floor, but it went round like a L-shape.
Altogether, they had about 38 apartments-- what they call apartments-- rooms, 38 rooms.
And what about toilet and washing?
Toilet was in the courtyard.
The water was obtainable from the well.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the-- and the rooms had nothing.
They were just bare rooms.
Cooking-- we had to get a little stove, one very ancient stove that could go in a museum and would fetch by today's standard an enormous sum in the antique market.
Oh, it was a real old piece of history, that.
So we were cooking.
Now, in order to get it installed-- as I said before, you see, the Poles were not doing any jobs for Jews-- there was someone living on-- in-- well, he was living in our courtyard.
He actually came from Warsaw.
He was, I think, an engineer before the war.
So he was able to erect a stove for us with a flue, like a pipe, a flue going outside, so that if we had any wood, or any coal, or whatever things, we could put it in, and it would give heat.
We could cook on it and at the same time obtain heat.
So when we came in the ghetto-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's before we came into the ghetto, we were still living in town, my father went to these secondhand shops, which the Poles already owned.
Before the war, it was owned by Jews.
So now, the Poles took over the Jewish businesses.
And he said to the Pole, I want this bed.
And the Pole asked for a price.
And he paid him, because he didn't want to argue the Poles.
They were very abusive.
And sometimes, they say, we don't sell to Jews.
So-- so we got a bed.
We bought an old wardrobe, also very ancient, also a museum piece-- a very, very big one.
The bed-- then we had to buy an iron bed for my brother.
And I think we had a small table and three chairs.
So all this had to be transported from the town into the ghetto.
And I think this happened-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one Jewish guy who was a friend of a friend of my father's undertook to do the transportation, provided we helped.
So we all help to load the bed, to load the wardrobe, and the other bed, and the table, the three chairs.
And from the town, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we walked, pushing that little-- that little van-- not a-- not a driven van, you know, because there was no horse, just four wheels.
And this was put on the top.
And we pushed it right up to our place.
And of course, all the neighbors came out.

They wanted to see who were the new people who moved in and what did they bring, because half of the people who already lived in that block, in that section where we were living, were people who lived there before or in the surrounding streets.
So they were, so to speak, the natives.
And we were the greenhorns who actually came.
So they sized up that, unfortunately, these people were as poor as a church mouse, just like them.
They had nothing-- only that ancient bed and the ancient wardrobe, because they had better wardrobes than that.
In fact, they said to us, from which museum [LAUGHS] did you get that?
So it was really a laughing stock at the time, but we took it in good spirit.
So we were already settled in that place.
Now, there was no lock on the door-- very strange.
You couldn't get a lock.
You couldn't get no locksmith.
So the engineer who installed our stove said, oh, I know what we could do.
We could put a hook on it.
And he looked around in his old tool shed.
And he found a hook.
And he fixed this hook for us.
So at night, we would put the hook in.
And this closed the door.
It gave a little privacy.
If somebody knocked, we would take the hook off of this.
But it's not that you sort of could lock.
Then that was in January.
Yes, we could still go out.
We still could go out.
Now, we had no money.
We had nothing, except that we had the tobacco and the cigarettes, which we traded in.
So we got a little bit of currency.
And we were really going very, very sparsely.
German Marks, German Marks.
Now, my father thought it was a good idea to build up stock of tobacco and cigarettes, because Jewish people found it very difficult to get to cigarettes and tobacco.
So I was dressed up with a black cap and German swastika.
And I went into town.
This was at the time when the ghetto was still open and you could buy.
And the law at the time wasn't like here in this country, that you couldn't buy unless you were over 16 years of age.
There, you see, you-- you could buy anything, because you could go on the errand of your grandfather or on your father.
And the tobacco and the spirit was under the Polish monopoly system.
And provided you paid, you got whatever you wanted.
So I got some cigarettes.
I got tobacco.
Then I went to the liquor store, and I bought vodka, because vodka, you see, everybody drinks.
And this was very good currency.
Wines, beers, nobody drank-- only the vodka.
So I brought this home.
And we kept this.
And I made as many trips as I could, because as we sold it, we got more money.
And we were about to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] build up our stocks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They try to divert the tram car going through certain streets from the ghetto.
So what they done is they divided the ghetto in two sections.
The main road was the border, where the tram car went through from one part of town to the other part of the town.
And on both sides, the ghetto was fenced in with barbed wire.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So in order to go onto the second side, we had to go over a wooden bridge.
That was to the left, and to the right as well, and the bridge on the top.
So if you want to go from the left over to the right or from the right over to the left, you had to use the bridge.

And they had three gates on the same main road for goods to go through with the authority of a German guard, like you wanted to bring in machinery, which of course, you couldn't carry over the bridge.
But the best way was to transport it from-- through a gate.
Then the German guard would come and would assess.
But this was still before the ghetto was hermetically sealed.
So they were erecting it.
And they had very big posters saying danger, typhoid.
Keep away.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] They didn't actually say ghetto.
They said Jewish inhabitation-- Jewish habitation, like [GERMAN].
That was the name-- [GERMAN] and danger to keep away.

Although the Jewish people didn't have typhoid at the time, because the ghetto was still open, they already didn't [? wanted ?] the Poles to come near, or any Germans who were thinking in assisting Jews.
So they kept them out.
The ghetto was closed, hermetically sealed off, on the first of May.
Hermetically sealed just means that there was no contact with the outer world.
The ghetto was sealed.
They had the guards.
Every few meters, they had a guard.
The guards were the ethnic Germans.
Later on, they were changed over.
They had the Ukrainian guards.
Then they had Latvian guards.
So we had a few change of guards all the time.
They were never the same.
And you couldn't leave the ghetto.
You couldn't leave the ghetto.
You couldn't come in to the ghetto.
So we were just on our own.
In May, it was very hot.
People had nothing to do, just come out.
So they were talking about starting to work, because a ghetto was like a little Jewish government.
And people had to do the cleaning for sanitation, for the removal of certain goods.
We had to work on the rails.
This was forced.
The Germans would pick up, you know, randomly in the street and put people to work.
I was picked up and was given a shovel.
And we had to get the rail section from one side of the road over to the others, meaning that the tram car that was entering the ghetto, or the main road of the ghetto, to go through could not shunt.
Shunting means when the rail goes from one to the other.
So here, it had to go straight.
So the shunting had to be diverted, had to be taken off.
And the shunting was done outside-- outside the ghetto.
And this was just to let the tram car go through the ghetto, because there was the bridge already.
And we could go over the bridge.
Poles-- Polish tram car drivers.
They went through, and the people were inside.
They could see into the ghetto.
In the beginning, they said, whenever a tram car approaches, you must make yourself scarce.
Otherwise, you will be shot dead.
You're not allowed to see them.
So each time a tram car came through, we had to make ourselves scarce-- run into one of the [INAUDIBLE] or to one of the houses.
Then the tram car only went into two directions, like it went to one street which was called [POLISH] Street.
And the other one was called [POLISH] Street.
These were the two main thoroughfares where the tram cars went through.
It was really very strange, because our street was one side of that road.
And my aunt was living across the road.
So if I wanted to go to her, I couldn't cross the road, because it was barbed wire on both sides.
So really, the only way to get to her was going straight down the road, turning around where the church was, and going over the bridge in order to walk back to come to her.
That's how it had to be.
Now, this-- the church-- which all the churches in Poland were Catholic churches-- this was a very big one.
And this was turned into a warehouse.
And around the corner from the church was the Kripol, which means the criminal police.
That's where Jews who had any valuables found in their houses had to appear there.
If they were not beaten to death, they were killed by sadistic ethnic Germans in the presence of a German Gestapo officer.
I haven't witnessed it.
But people who were living in our block and had hidden gold, like jewelry and other things which were found, had to make an appearance.
And they almost lost their life.
They came back, and they told us.
Well, we were still together.
We were still together.
We were talking now about 1940.
So we were still together.
And we had to work.
So I worked on the-- on the rail-- on the tram car rail.
Then they said, oh, you're already working in the construction unit.
There is another rail which we got to lay.
And this was on another street, not very far from we were living, to afford the tram car to get from outside the ghetto with loaded wagons to come inside the ghetto.
And it was like this, which was also a strange to us, but we find this only out later on, usually, in a tram car, it's about two or three sections where passengers go on and off.
But you can also put a wagon and couple it to a tram car.
And provided it's on the rails and the dimensions are in comparison to that of the rail, it will go through just like a ordinary train.

So once we finished constructing that rail so there was the link from the railway section outside the ghetto to transfer the goods onto the tram car section, and it went right through a very, very long road into the ghetto.
And this was very useful.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
